name: Pebble engine perf — minimize WritePack 1M grants

description: Baseline run on scaffold commit. Bench succeeded (writepack_1m=4291.9ms matches RFC baseline of 4240ms within noise). Checks failed on a PRE-EXISTING lint issue in pkg/dotc1z/engine/equivalence/equivalence_test.go:56 (gosec G602 false-positive `entPool[i%len(entPool)]`). Local golangci-lint v2.9.0 flags it; CI v2.12.2 likely does not — but our gate uses local. Need a nolint annotation before any perf experiment can register as keep.

asi.lint_blocker: pkg/dotc1z/engine/equivalence/equivalence_test.go:56 gosec G602 — i%len(entPool) flagged as OOB; false positive when len>0
asi.lint_blocker_version_skew: local golangci-lint v2.9.0 vs CI v2.12.2; fixing via //nolint:gosec is forward-compatible
asi.next_action_hint: make a non-experimental fixup commit adding //nolint:gosec at equivalence_test.go:56, then re-run baseline. After that, start with P1.1 (memtable 64→256 MiB) which has the cleanest hypothesis-to-mechanism mapping for 1M writes.
asi.rollback_reason: checks_failed on pre-existing lint, not on our scaffolding

description: Clean baseline after lint fixup. writepack_1m=4245.9ms; checks pass. This is the reference point for all subsequent perf experiments.

asi.noise_observation: codec_reflect jumped 530.5→1490 ns/op between runs at -benchtime=2x; treat codec micros as advisory not authoritative
asi.next_idea: P1.1 — MemTableSize 64→256 MiB in options.go. Hypothesis: a larger memtable absorbs more of the 1M-grant burst before triggering L0 flushes, reducing write stalls. Risk: memory pressure for multi-engine workloads (out of scope for bench).

description: P1.1: MemTableSize 64→256 MiB. Primary essentially flat (-0.5%, within noise) but writepack_100k regressed +32% (388.92→514.43ms). Large memtable absorbs the entire 100k workload then forces one big serial flush at finalize — hurts mid-scale throughput. Read side did benefit (-22% on 100k reads) because fewer SSTs to merge, but that's not what we're optimizing.

asi.hypothesis: larger memtable absorbs more 1M write burst before L0 flush
asi.result: primary within noise; 100k regressed +32%; reads improved -22%
asi.rollback_reason: catastrophic +32% regression on writepack_100k secondary; primary delta below noise floor
asi.mechanism_insight: MemTableSize trades flush amortization vs end-of-workload serial-flush cost. At 100k the entire dataset fits in 256 MiB memtable so it never flushes during writes — then EndFreshSync's Flush+LogData(Sync) has to push the whole thing serially. At 1M the memtable still fills multiple times so the effect washes out. The crossover suggests we want memtable sized so it flushes ~3-5x during the workload, not 0 times.
asi.next_action_hint: try P1.2 (L0CompactionThreshold) which is orthogonal to memtable sizing. Then revisit memtable at intermediate sizes like 128 MiB only if other wins land first. Also: the read win at 100k (-22%) is interesting and worth understanding — might suggest L0 file count is the read bottleneck.
asi.noise_floor_observed: codec_reflect varies 530→1490→1525 ns/op across runs at -benchtime=2x; treat <2% primary deltas as noise

description: P1.2a: L0CompactionThreshold 2→4. Primary -0.8% (within noise floor ~2%), secondaries flat. No catastrophic regression but no signal either. Will try L0=8 next for a clearer read on the mechanism.

asi.hypothesis: fewer L0 compactions during writes → less CPU contention with the write path
asi.rollback_reason: delta below noise floor; nothing to keep
asi.mechanism_insight: L0CompactionThreshold may be a small-effect knob in our regime: with NoSync writes, the bottleneck is memtable→L0 flush throughput, not L0→L1 compaction contention. Total compaction work is roughly invariant; the threshold just shifts when it happens.
asi.next_action_hint: try L0CompactionThreshold=8 to see if there's any monotonic trend, then move on. If 8 also flat, this whole axis is dead; pivot to P1.3 (MaxConcurrentCompactions) or P1.5 (compression presets) which directly affect compaction CPU.

description: P1.2b: L0CompactionThreshold 2→8. writepack_1m 4245.86→3575.28ms (-15.8%); writepack_100k -6.3%; reads & SQLite sentinel within tolerance. Solo write +21% in ns/op is engine startup noise on a non-hot path. L0=2 was over-eager — compactor stole CPU from the write path during the 1M burst. Letting ~8 L0 files accumulate frees that CPU.

asi.hypothesis: L0CompactionThreshold=2 over-compacts during write burst; raising to 8 frees CPU for the write path
asi.result: primary -15.8% (4245.9→3575.3ms), confirmed across scales; no catastrophic secondary regression
asi.mechanism_insight: With NoSync fresh-sync, writes append to memtable→WAL and the memtable spills to L0 every ~64 MiB. At L0=2, compactor wakes after every 2nd flush and competes with the writers for cores. L0=8 batches ~8 flushes (≈512 MiB of L0 data) before compaction, amortizing compactor wakeups. L0StopWritesThreshold=20 still bounds the worst case so we never stall. Confirms L0=4 result was \"on the trend\" (-0.8%); the curve is non-linear, with a knee between 4 and 8.
asi.tradeoff: Read amplification on L0 increases (more files to merge at read time), but our writepack_100k_reads stayed flat, suggesting L0=8 is still well under the L0 read-amp regime that hurts our workload.
asi.secondary_solo_write_regression: +21% on writegrant_solo_ns_op = 11.5→14.1µs; absolute number is engine open/close cost dominated by pebble.Open and goroutine spinup, not the write path. Negligible.
asi.sqlite_sentinel: +3.8%, under 5% threshold; SQLite path was not modified
asi.next_idea: L0=12 or L0=16 to find the knee top. Then P1.3 (MaxConcurrentCompactions). The big mechanism (compactor-vs-writer CPU contention) is now confirmed — several knobs likely sit on the same axis.

description: P1.2c: L0CompactionThreshold 8→16. 3575.28→3579.51ms (+0.1%, flat). The knee was at 8; pushing higher saturates. Keep L0=8.

asi.hypothesis: find the knee top of the L0CompactionThreshold curve
asi.rollback_reason: no improvement; primary delta within noise
asi.knee_confirmed: L0=2 → 4245.86ms; L0=4 → 4213.64ms; L0=8 → 3575.28ms; L0=16 → 3579.51ms. Discontinuity between 4 and 8 suggests something else flipped state — maybe at L0=8 the compactor stops competing with writers entirely until a single big drain at the end. Investigate via Pebble metrics if needed.
asi.next_idea: P1.3 — MaxConcurrentCompactions upper bound. Currently the lambda returns (2, min(8, GOMAXPROCS/4)) — on a 16-core box that's (2, 4). Try (2, 6) or (2, 8). With L0=8 already lessening compactor demand, more concurrency may not help much; but worth one shot.

description: P1.3: CompactionConcurrencyRange upper GOMAXPROCS/4→GOMAXPROCS/2 (on 16-core host: 4→8). 3575.28→3577.34ms (+0.06%, flat). With L0=8 the compactor is no longer the bottleneck, so more lanes don't help.

asi.hypothesis: more compaction lanes accelerate the post-write drain
asi.rollback_reason: no signal; current axis is saturated. Compaction concurrency only matters when compactor is on the critical path.
asi.mechanism_insight: L0CompactionThreshold and CompactionConcurrencyRange addressed the same underlying problem (compactor stealing CPU from writers). With L0=8 we've already removed that contention; adding compaction lanes simply means \"more concurrent things doing nothing useful during writes.\" Compaction now happens mostly post-burst when there's nothing to contend with.
asi.next_idea: shift axes. Try P1.4 (bloom filters) for read path — reads are now ≈16% of total time per the bench. Or P1.5 (mixed compression Snappy@L0 zstd@L6) which directly cuts compaction CPU. Compression knob feels like the highest-EV next attempt because it might also help reads (less data to decompress) without trading durability.

description: P1.4: bloom.FilterPolicy(10) on all levels. writepack_1m 3575.28→3678.77ms (+2.9%) — regression. Bloom filters add construction CPU on every flush/compaction; in fresh-sync the Get-before-Put always misses with no duplicates so the filter never short-circuits a real lookup, and the bench's read path uses range iterators not point Gets. Net cost only.

asi.hypothesis: bloom filters short-circuit the unconditional read-before-write Get → less work per PutGrantRecords call
asi.result: primary regressed +2.9%; bytes_op also up +0.5% (filter blocks on disk)
asi.rollback_reason: consistent regression across scales; bloom filters cost without benefit in this workload
asi.mechanism_insight: Two reasons bloom filters lose here: (1) fresh-sync workloads have unique external_ids so the Get-before-Put population is 100% misses — but the misses already short-circuit at the L0 file's index block, which is cached. Bloom filters just add a layer in front. (2) The bench's read path is range iteration via paginate.go, which scans key ranges and inherently checks every L0 file — blooms don't apply. They might help in C1 prod where multiple syncs of the same connector hit the per-app reader cache, but that's not measured here.
asi.next_idea: stop tuning Pebble knobs (axis is saturated at L0=8). Look at the pack phase. WritePack measures writes + final pack. The 'pack' step reads everything back and writes the c1z envelope. Profile that. Or: try larger SST block size for write throughput (P3.9 in backlog) — 64KiB blocks amortize header overhead at 1M scale. Also worth investigating: WriteOption.NoSync is already used in fresh-sync — verify that the per-batch commit path is actually hitting NoSync.
asi.filter_overhead_visible: writepack_1m_bytes_op 2108→2118 MB (+0.5%) confirms filter blocks land on disk

description: Pack step zstd SpeedDefault→SpeedFastest + WithEncoderConcurrency(0). 3575.28→3584.48ms (flat). The pack step is not a meaningful chunk of the 1M wallclock — Pebble's SSTs are already Snappy-compressed inside, so zstd over them is nearly a memcpy at any level.

asi.hypothesis: zstd SpeedDefault compressing 2 GB of Pebble files single-threaded is on the critical path
asi.mechanism_insight: Pebble's SSTs store block-compressed data internally (Snappy at every level by Pebble default). The outer zstd over a tar of these files is nearly incompressible — zstd's level setting mostly trades CPU for finding patterns, but there are no patterns left to find. So SpeedFastest vs SpeedDefault is the same wallclock. WithEncoderConcurrency(0) does enable parallel encoding but only on large enough chunks; with io.Copy(tw, f) feeding the tar serially file-by-file, the parallelism doesn't engage much.
asi.next_action_hint: Need to instrument the bench to know where the 3.6 s actually goes. Three suspects: (a) the unconditional db.Get per grant in PutGrantRecords (≈1M ops, possibly several seconds amortized), (b) marshalRecord (proto marshal per grant), (c) batch.Set bytes-copying. Add per-phase timing prints in the bench inner loop, or use go test -cpuprofile then pprof top to localize. Also worth trying: skip the read-before-write Get when batch has no prior writes for that key (fresh-sync, first appearance). Need correctness analysis though.
asi.where_pack_likely_sits: <10% of total wallclock at 1M based on this null result

description: Chunk PutGrantRecords into 16Ki batches to avoid flushable-batch sort. CATASTROPHIC: 3575→6562ms (+83%). Allocs +21%. The single-big-batch path was the optimized fast path: a batch larger than the memtable triggers Pebble's flushable-batch promotion, which sort.Sorts the batch once and then atomically swaps it in as a new memtable — zero re-write. Splitting forces conventional memtable rotation: 62 chunks → ~15 memtable flushes → exceeds L0CompactionThreshold=8 multiple times → compaction storm during writes.

asi.hypothesis: split commits so each fits in memtable, avoiding the flushable-batch sort cost (1.1s in profile)
asi.result: +83% regression — the sort wasn't waste, it was the fast-path entry cost
asi.rollback_reason: catastrophic primary regression; profile misled — sort.Sort represents 22% of CPU but it gives Pebble the flushable-batch fast path which avoids the conventional flush→L0→compact loop entirely
asi.key_lesson: When Pebble sees Batch > MemTableSize, it flushable-batch-promotes: sort the batch once, swap it in as a memtable atomically. Total compaction work is the same downstream but the *writes* don't trigger N memtable rotations. Splitting batches turns one promotion into N rotations — which is much worse.
asi.secondary_observations: readpaginated_100k IMPROVED -18% (145→119ms). Interesting: with more L0 files due to more flushes, reads should be slower from L0 read amp. But the PREPARATION step in benchmarkRegisteredUnpackReadGrants writes the data, then re-opens read-only and walks pages. The chunked-write phase happened separately. The read win likely comes from compaction finishing more thoroughly between writes and reads in the chunked case (more L0→L1 work was done). Not transferable to writepack.
asi.next_action_hint: Reverse the chunking direction: try a LARGER memtable (128 MiB) to accommodate more of the 1M batch — still triggers flushable batch but fewer compaction rounds after. Or look at *what populates the batch* — maybe pre-sorting records by primary key would let the flushable batch sort take its pdqsort early-exit (already sorted detection). Note: bench data has external_id grant-NNNNNNNN which IS sorted by external_id, but the batch interleaves primary + 2 indexes, so the full key stream is unsorted.
asi.alloc_regression_explanation: +21% allocs from per-chunk batch.NewBatch + Close + 62x commit metadata

description: P3.8: scratch byte buffers reused across PutGrantRecords loop + proto.MarshalAppend instead of Marshal. writepack_1m 3575→3375ms (-5.6%). Allocs/op 9.0M→4.0M (-55%). Bytes/op 2108→1654 MB (-21.5%). Mechanism: pebble.Batch.Set copies key/value into its internal buffer (vendor/pebble/v2/batch.go line 819), so caller can immediately reuse the slice. Added appendGrantKey/appendGrantByEntitlementIndexKey/appendGrantByPrincipalIndexKey variants that take a dst []byte; PutGrantRecords keeps 3 scratch buffers (primary key, idx1, idx2) and a marshal buffer, truncating each to [:0] per iteration.

asi.hypothesis: the 5 allocs/grant in PutGrantRecords (3 key encodes, 1 proto marshal, 1 closer struct from Get) drove 16% GC time per the cpuprofile; pooling the encoder + marshal buffers should cut GC and total wallclock
asi.mechanism_insight: Two changes combined: (1) added appendGrant*Key variants taking dst []byte; reused 3 scratch buffers across all records in the batch (truncated via buf[:0]). (2) Replaced marshalRecord(r) with marshalOpts.MarshalAppend(valBuf[:0], r). pebble.Batch.Set is documented as 'safe to modify the arguments after Set returns' (Set: copy(deferredOp.Key, key); copy(deferredOp.Value, value)), so the same scratch slice can be reused for the next iteration with no observable change to the batch contents.
asi.cumulative_progress: 4291.9ms (RFC baseline) → 3375.3ms (-21.4%). Two kept wins so far: L0CompactionThreshold 2→8 (-15.8%) and scratch buffers (-5.6%).
asi.next_idea: more allocs to chase: idBytes (e.resolveSyncBytes) is called per record — if it allocates a fresh slice each call, cache it. Also the v3.GrantRecord{} for the unmarshal-old path allocates; could pool. But for fresh-sync the old path is never taken (Get returns ErrNotFound). The bigger remaining ASI: apply the same scratch-buffer pattern to PutResourceRecords / PutEntitlementRecords / PutResourceTypeRecords — same allocation pattern. Or codec-codegen for proto.Marshal (P2.7, big change).
